summary: Kenneth Suna explains that cash-secured puts and covered calls are not gambling when executed with proper risk understanding. The key distinction: they become gambling only if the trader ignores downside risk, chases quick profits, or lacks understanding of assignment mechanics and capital requirements.
takeaways: Covered calls: sell shares you already own at a strike price (e.g., bought at $50, sell calls at $52) and keep the premium regardless of outcome
Cash-secured puts require full cash reserve in account (e.g., $4,700 cash to sell puts on 100 shares at $47 strike)
Assignment on cash-secured puts is mandatory if stock falls below strike at expiration (e.g., forced to buy at $47 even if stock is $42)
You can buy back (close) put contracts early to avoid assignment, though you may pay extra to exit the trade
Cash-secured puts and covered calls are income strategies on intended positions, not gambling, if you understand and respect downside risk
These strategies become gambling if: you ignore downside risk, chase quick profits, don't understand assignment mechanics, or bet without position intention
Weekly expiration cycles mentioned as timeframe example for covered calls

strategy_candidates.change: Implement hard rule requiring $4,700+ cash reserve per 100-share CSP contract on Premium Desk to ensure assignment capacity
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth emphasizes 'you must have that cash on hand' and explicitly states the cash requirement ($4,700 for $47 strike on 100 shares), contrasting uninformed trading with disciplined execution

strategy_candidates.change: Add early buy-to-close rule: close profitable CSP contracts at 50% of max profit before expiration to reduce assignment risk
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth notes ability to 'buy back the options contract' and exit trades early when underlying moves against the position, reducing forced assignment risk

title: Are cash secured puts gambling?
